tool.name: GaCDI Data Commons Downloader
tool.description: acquire data from the NCI Genomic (GDC) or Proteomic (PDC) Data Commons

param.name: manifest_file
param.label: Manifest file from the GDC or PDC portal
param.help: The source is detected automatically from the manifest header. PDC file manifests may be CSV or TSV; GDC manifests are TSV.

tool.help:
**What it does**
Downloads files listed in a manifest exported by either the NCI `Genomic Data Commons (GDC) <https://portal.gdc.cancer.gov/>`_ or `Proteomic Data Commons (PDC) <https://pdc.cancer.gov/>`_. The data commons is detected automatically from the manifest header; an unrecognized or ambiguous file fails with a message that identifies the observed and expected columns.
**GDC manifests**
Export a download manifest from the GDC portal. Open-access data needs no token. For controlled-access GDC data, store the token in Galaxy's optional **GDC credentials** entry. The token is injected only for a GDC job and is passed to ``gdc-client`` through a mode-0600 named pipe, never a command-line argument or regular file.
**PDC manifests**
In PDC, open **Explore**, apply filters, select files on the **Files** tab, and choose **Export File Manifest** in CSV or TSV format. PDC authorization is encoded in each manifest's signed download link, so the GDC credential is not used. PDC links expire after seven days (168 hours); re-export the file manifest if the job reports an expired URL. PDC also limits repeated downloads of the same file from one IP address to 10 attempts in 24 hours.
Downloaded PDC files are checked against both the size and MD5 checksum in the manifest. A rerun skips already-complete files and safely resumes the remaining rows.
**Output**
A collection containing every file listed in the manifest, ready to feed straight into an analysis pipeline.
Elements keep the file name published by the commons, so a collection element reads the same as the manifest row it came from. Formats that Galaxy models are typed on arrival rather than sniffed:
========================== ==================== ========================================== Published as Galaxy datatype Typical use ========================== ==================== ========================================== ``.mzML`` / ``.mzML.gz`` ``mzml`` spectra for Comet, MS-GF+, OpenMS, MaxQuant ``.mzXML`` ``mzxml`` legacy open spectra ``.mgf`` ``mgf`` peak lists ``.raw`` ``thermo.raw`` Thermo native, input to msconvert ``.mzid`` / ``.mzid.gz`` ``mzid`` peptide-spectrum match identifications ``.pepXML`` / ``.protXML`` ``pepxml``/``protxml`` search and inference results ``.psm``, ``.tsv``, ``.txt`` ``tabular`` PSM tables, clinical and metadata files ========================== ==================== ==========================================
Anything else is kept as ``data``: still downloadable, still usable, just not typed. No file listed in the manifest is ever dropped from the collection.
**Compressed files are expanded for you**
The commons publish several formats gzipped. Galaxy's ``mzml`` and ``mzid`` datatypes describe uncompressed XML, so gzipped downloads would not be accepted by downstream proteomics tools without a manual conversion step. This tool verifies each download against the manifest's size and MD5 and *then* expands it, so ``06CPTAC_C_GBM_W_PNNL_20210830_B2S1_f08.mzML.gz`` arrives in your history as a usable ``mzml`` dataset.
Formats that are compressed by design keep their compression: BAM, BGZF-indexed VCF, tabix indexes, and FASTQ are never expanded.
**Building a pipeline from here**
A typical reprocessing route is ``.raw`` → msconvert → ``mzml`` → a search engine (Comet, MS-GF+, SearchGUI) against a FASTA with decoys → FDR control → protein inference and quantification. To start from the commons' own identifications instead, take the ``mzid`` or ``.psm`` elements directly into filtering and statistics. Note that a manifest exported without care can mix fractions, batches and studies; check that the files you select form a coherent set before searching.
